full_response:
We label every walk to the library as a “trip’’ (cost 1).  
At the start of the semester neither student has any book.

──────────────────────────────────  
Cam’s “hold-as-long-as-possible’’ strategy (k = 2)

Most-recently-checked-out book is shown in bold.

Reading  | Cam already has? | Action taken                         | Books after reading | Trips so far
---------|------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------|-------------
a        | no               | check out a                          | **a**               | 1
b        | no               | check out b                          | a, **b**            | 2
c        | no               | return **b**, check out c            | a, **c**            | 3
d        | no               | return **c**, check out d            | a, **d**            | 4
d        | yes              | nothing                              | a, **d**            | 4
c        | no               | return **d**, check out c            | a, **c**            | 5

Cam walks to the library 5 times.

──────────────────────────────────  
Opal’s optimal (offline) behaviour (k = 2)

Opal knows the whole sequence and can apply Belady’s “farthest-in-the-future’’ rule.

Reading  | Books before | Which book (if any) is returned? | Books after | Trips so far | Reason
---------|--------------|----------------------------------|-------------|--------------|-------
a        | ∅            | —                                | a           | 1            | first appearance of a
b        | a            | —                                | a, b        | 2            | first appearance of b
c        | a, b         | a or b (neither used again)      | b, c        | 3            | evict a (arbitrary)
d        | b, c         | b (never used again)             | c, d        | 4            | keep c (needed later)
d        | c, d         | —                                | c, d        | 4            | already have d
c        | c, d         | —                                | c, d        | 4            | already have c

Opal walks to the library only 4 times.  
This is optimal: the sequence contains four distinct titles (a, b, c, d) and the students start with none, so at least four trips are unavoidable.

──────────────────────────────────  
\boxed{\text{Cam makes 5 trips;\; Opal (optimal) makes 4 trips.}}

Confidence: 0.35
